raw_case:
Endoscopic Ultrasound-Guided Diagnosis of Gallbladder Mixed Neuroendocrine Non-Neuroendocrine Tumor With an Anomalous Pancreaticobiliary Junction


ABSTRACT

Mixed neuroendocrine non-neuroendocrine neoplasms are exceedingly rare tumors, especially those of gallbladder origin. Anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction is an uncommon congenital abnormality that can predispose various types of hepatobiliary malignancies. We present an unusual case of a 46-year-old woman with anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction who presented to the emergency department with nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a gallbladder mass concerning for primary malignancy and liver lesions. Endoscopic ultrasound and fine-needle biopsy were valuable diagnostic tools used to sample the gallbladder, liver lesions, and common hepatic duct under one minimally invasive procedure. Pathology showed a gallbladder mixed neuroendocrine non-neuroendocrine neoplasms, with neuroendocrine components in the liver and adenocarcinoma components in the common hepatic duct.

INTRODUCTION

Mixed neuroendocrine non-neuroendocrine neoplasms (MiNENs) are a pathologically heterogenous group of tumors defined by the World Health Organization as mixed neoplasms of gastroenterohepatic origin with neuroendocrine and non-neuroendocrine components. These are a rare form of gallbladder malignancy.1,2 The majority of literature on gallbladder MiNEN stems from case reports making true incidence unknown, but the 2008 Surveillance of Rare Cancers in Europe registry approximates MiNEN incidence as <0.01/100,000 cases per year.2 An anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction (APBJ) is a rare congenital malformation associated with hepatobiliary and pancreatic disorders such as choledochal cysts, choledocholithiasis, gallbladder carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, and recurrent pancreatitis.3 It is estimated that approximately 10% of patients with APBJ will develop gallbladder carcinoma over the course of their lifetime.3,4 We present an unusual case of a gallbladder mass with liver lesions and common hepatic duct (CHD) stricture with subsequent endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography sampling which revealed MiNEN with metastatic neuroendocrine tumors in the liver and adenocarcinoma in the CHD in a patient with an APBJ.

CASE REPORT

A 42-year-old African American woman with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension presented to the emergency department with 1-month history of worsening nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, and 10 pounds of unintentional weight loss. Liver function tests were elevated: aspartate aminotransferase 430 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 575 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 459 U/L, and total bilirubin 1.7 mg/dL. Tumor markers were sent and notable for elevation of both carcinoembryonic antigen (225 ng/mL) and CA19-9 (183.9 U/mL). Magnetic resonance imaging of the abdomen and pelvis revealed an enhancing nodular mass at the fundus of the gallbladder concerning for primary malignancy (Figure 1). Also noted were multifocal liver lesions, infiltration of the porta hepatis, and necrotic retroperitoneal lymph nodes concerning for metastatic disease (Figure 1). Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) confirmed the gallbladder mass in addition to a biliary stricture (Figure 1).EUS and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography were subsequently performed. EUS demonstrated a 20 mm × 15 mm heterogenous, hypoechoic mass in the neck of the gallbladder with irregular outer margins and likely invasion of the hepatic parenchyma. The right lobe of the liver contained round, homogenous, and hyperechoic lesions suggestive of metastases, with the largest measuring 8 mm (Figure 2). Fine-needle biopsy was performed for both the gallbladder and hepatic masses (Figure 2). Cholangiogram was notable for a 1 cm stricture in the CHD and a Komi classification type IIA APBJ. Cholangioscopy performed revealed narrowing at the CHD and associated erythema, congestion, and increased vascularization concerning for malignant stricture. Biopsies were taken from the CHD for pathology, and brushings were obtained for cytology. A plastic biliary stent was placed in the common bile duct at the end of the procedure.Pathology revealed 2 unique populations of tumor cells (Figure 3). This confirmed the diagnosis of a MiNEN in the gallbladder, with neuroendocrine tumors in the liver, and adenocarcinoma in the CHD. The patient has started chemotherapy with carboplatin and etoposide.

DISCUSSION

APBJ is a rare congenital malformation wherein the pancreatic and bile ducts join to form a common channel outside of the duodenal wall. The reflux of pancreatic enzymes into the biliary tract causes increased hyperplastic changes in the gallbladder and bile duct, and markedly increases rates of malignancy, predominantly adenocarcinoma.3,5–7 This also explains why APBJ is an independent risk factor of gallbladder malignancy due to the development of precursor neuroendocrine metaplasia from chronic inflammation.4 Prophylactic cholecystectomy is recommended for patients with APBJ to prevent the onset of malignant changes even without choledochal cyst.5,8 Rates of gallbladder malignancy from a nationwide survey in Japan ranged from 21.6% to as high as 42.4% in patients with dilated and non-dilated-type APBJ, respectively.5,9 This pathophysiology may have played a role in the development of our patient's gallbladder, liver, and CHD malignancies. APBJ-associated gallbladder carcinomas are also documented more often in younger, female patients compared with non-APBJ-associated cases.3Even more unusual are MiNENs arising in the setting of an APBJ, especially those that are of gallbladder origin. Although neuroendocrine differentiation of APBJ-associated tumors are not well documented, this case is consistent with previously reported demographics of APBJ-associated gallbladder carcinomas. The neuroendocrine components of MiNENs tend to display more aggressive behavior than non-neuroendocrine components and have a higher likelihood for metastasis.10,11 Neuroendocrine tumors of pancreaticobiliary origin most commonly metastasize to the liver, which is associated with a worse prognosis.12In diagnosing a MiNEN, immunohistochemical testing is imperative. Mucicarmine is highly specific for glandular epithelial mucin found in adenocarcinoma, and chromogranin and synaptophysin identify secretory vesicles seen in neuroendocrine tumors.13,14 In this case, immunohistochemistry determined 2 unique tumor cell differentiations in the gallbladder lesion. One component stained positive for mucicarmine and negative for synaptophysin, indicating mucinous adenocarcinoma (Figure 3). CAM5.2, CDX2, CK7 (patchy), and CK20 (patchy) were positive, suggesting primary gastrointestinal origin.15–17 The same morphology was found in the CHD stricture biopsy.In the other component of the gallbladder lesion, positive chromogranin, synaptophysin, CAM5.2, CK7, CK20, and CDX2 (patchy) staining confirmed neuroendocrine differentiation of gastrointestinal origin (Figure 3).15–17 Tumor cells in the liver stained positive for CK7, CK20, PAX8, chromogranin, and synaptophysin, also confirming neuroendocrine differentiation (Figure 3). This similar morphology determined the presence of a metastatic high-grade neuroendocrine neoplasm from the gallbladder. Ki-67 immunostaining of the neuroendocrine component in both the gallbladder and liver lesion showed more than 60% of cells with mitotic activity, indicative of a highly proliferative neuroendocrine neoplasm which correlates with a more aggressive clinical course.18Treatment recommendations for MiNENs follow standard of care for either neuroendocrine or adenocarcinoma components, but most cases target treatment against the neuroendocrine component due to often more aggressive behavior and poorer prognosis.2,19,20 Platinum-etoposide chemotherapy is recommended for gastrointestinal neuroendocrine tumors.21,22This case illustrates the value of endoscopic ultrasound-guided sampling as a minimally invasive, nonoperative approach in evaluating multiple hepatobiliary tract lesions in a single procedure, thereby minimizing complications and aiding in timely diagnosis.23,24 It is imperative in sampling multiple areas where lesions may be to confirm a more accurate diagnosis.

DISCLOSURES

Author contributions: E. Holzwanger performed the procedures. E. Tsuchiyose drafted the manuscript. M. Talanian and E. Holzwanger revised and reviewed the manuscript. H. Liao reviewed the pathology sections and prepared the pathology figure and legend. E. Holzwanger is the article guarantor.Financial disclosure: E. Holzwanger is a medical consultant for Boston Scientific. The remainder of authors have no conflicts of interest.Informed consent was obtained for this case report.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 42-year-old African American woman.
- **Chief Complaint**: Nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain, and unintentional weight loss over 1 month.
- **History of Present Illness**: Elevated liver function tests, weight loss of 10 pounds, and persistent abdominal symptoms.
- **Past Medical History**: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension.
- **Personal History**: Not provided.
- **Family History**: Not provided.
- **Physical Examination**: Not detailed in the report.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - **Laboratory**: Elevated liver enzymes (AST 430 U/L, ALT 575 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 459 U/L, total bilirubin 1.7 mg/dL), elevated tumor markers (carcinoembryonic antigen 225 ng/mL, CA19-9 183.9 U/mL).
  - **Imaging**:
    - MRI: Enhancing nodular gallbladder mass, multifocal liver lesions, porta hepatis infiltration, retroperitoneal lymph node necrosis, and gallbladder mass with biliary stricture.
    - MRCP: Confirmed gallbladder mass and biliary stricture.
  - **Endoscopic Procedures**:
    - EUS: Heterogeneous gallbladder mass with hepatic invasion, liver lesions suggestive of metastases.
    - Fine-needle biopsy: Obtained from gallbladder and liver lesions.
    - Cholangiogram: Komi type IIA anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction (APBJ) with a 1-cm stricture in the common hepatic duct.
    - Cholangioscopy: Malignant CHD stricture with associated erythema and congestion. Biopsies and cytology were taken.
  - **Pathology and Immunohistochemistry**:
    - Gallbladder: Mixed neuroendocrine and non-neuroendocrine neoplasm (MiNEN) with two tumor populations (mucinous adenocarcinoma and neuroendocrine differentiation).
    - CHD: Adenocarcinoma.
    - Liver: Metastatic high-grade neuroendocrine tumor.
    - Ki-67 staining: High mitotic activity (>60%) indicating aggressive neuroendocrine neoplasm.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Possibilities**: Based on the symptoms (nausea, vomiting, RUQ pain, weight loss) and imaging findings (gallbladder mass, liver lesions, CHD stricture), the primary concerns included gallbladder carcinoma, metastatic disease, cholangiocarcinoma, and other hepatobiliary malignancies.
2. **APBJ as a Risk Factor**: The congenital malformation (APBJ) predisposed the patient to hepatobiliary malignancies and partially explained the development of the gallbladder mass and biliary abnormalities.
3. **Confirmatory Pathology**:
   - Biopsy and immunohistochemical staining provided evidence for two distinct neoplastic components: neuroendocrine and adenocarcinomatous, fulfilling the diagnostic criteria for MiNEN.
   - Liver biopsies confirmed metastatic neuroendocrine differentiation, and the CHD biopsy showed adenocarcinoma.

Other entities, such as pure neuroendocrine tumors, adenocarcinomas, or cholangiocarcinoma alone, were ruled out since these do not present with dual tumor components as observed in this case. The diagnosis was refined by histopathology and immunohistochemical markers.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Gallbladder mixed neuroendocrine-non-neuroendocrine neoplasm (MiNEN) associated with an anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction, with metastasis to the liver (neuroendocrine component) and adenocarcinoma in the common hepatic duct.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Gallbladder MiNEN with liver metastasis and CHD adenocarcinoma in the setting of APBJ.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze Chief Complaint and Initial Findings:** The patient presents with nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain, unintentional weight loss, and elevated liver function tests. These are concerning symptoms and signs suggestive of hepatobiliary disease, potentially malignancy. The weight loss and persistent abdominal symptoms further raise suspicion for a chronic and potentially serious condition.

<step 2> **Evaluate Laboratory Results:** Markedly elevated liver enzymes (AST, ALT, ALP) and mildly elevated bilirubin indicate hepatocellular and cholestatic liver injury.  Significantly elevated tumor markers (CEA and CA19-9) are highly suggestive of an underlying malignancy, particularly in the gastrointestinal or hepatobiliary system.

<step 3> **Interpret Imaging Findings (MRI & MRCP):** The MRI and MRCP reveal a gallbladder mass, multifocal liver lesions, porta hepatis infiltration, retroperitoneal lymph node necrosis, and biliary stricture. These findings are strongly indicative of advanced malignancy with local invasion, distant metastases to the liver and lymph nodes, and biliary obstruction due to the mass and stricture.

<step 4> **Assess Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS) and Cholangiogram/Cholangioscopy Results:** EUS confirms gallbladder mass with hepatic invasion and liver lesions consistent with metastases, reinforcing the advanced stage of the disease. The cholangiogram identifies an anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction (APBJ), which is a known risk factor for biliary cancers. The cholangioscopy confirms a malignant stricture in the common hepatic duct (CHD).

<step 5> **Review Pathology and Immunohistochemistry Reports:** The pathology reports are definitive.
    * **Gallbladder:**  Diagnosis of a Mixed Neuroendocrine and Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm (MiNEN), specifically composed of mucinous adenocarcinoma and neuroendocrine differentiation. This is a rare and aggressive type of tumor.
    * **CHD:** Adenocarcinoma.
    * **Liver:** Metastatic high-grade neuroendocrine tumor, confirming liver metastases are from the neuroendocrine component of the gallbladder MiNEN.
    * **Ki-67:** High proliferation index (>60%) in the neuroendocrine component signifies a highly aggressive tumor behavior.

<step 6> **Synthesize all Findings for Final Diagnosis:** Integrating all the data points, the patient has a complex and advanced malignancy. The primary tumor is a Mixed Neuroendocrine and Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm (MiNEN) of the gallbladder.  The neuroendocrine component of this MiNEN has metastasized to the liver.  Additionally, there is a separate adenocarcinoma identified in the common hepatic duct, which could be part of the MiNEN spectrum or a concurrent primary tumor.  The overall clinical picture is consistent with advanced biliary tract cancer originating from the gallbladder with metastatic spread and biliary obstruction.

<step 7> **Formulate the Final Diagnostic Statement:** Based on the comprehensive analysis, the final diagnosis should encompass all pathological findings and the extent of disease.  It is crucial to highlight the MiNEN of the gallbladder, its metastatic nature (specifically the neuroendocrine component to the liver), and the concurrent adenocarcinoma of the CHD.

### Answer:
Metastatic Mixed Neuroendocrine and Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm (MiNEN) of the Gallbladder (Mucinous Adenocarcinoma and Neuroendocrine Differentiation) with Liver Metastasis of High-Grade Neuroendocrine Tumor Component and Concurrent Adenocarcinoma of the Common Hepatic Duct.
